records.ety: [Sp. don; akin to Pg. dom, It. donno; fr. L. dominus master. See Dame, and cf. Domine, Dominie, Domino, Dan, Dom.]

defs: Sir; Mr; Signior; -- a title in Spain, formerly given to noblemen and gentlemen only, but now common to all classes.
quotes: Don is used in Italy, though not so much as in Spain. France talks of Dom Calmet, England of Dan Lydgate.

defs: A grand personage, or one making pretension to consequence; especially, the head of a college, or one of the fellows at the English universities.

records.ety: [Do + on; -- opposed to doff. See Do, v. t., 7.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Donned (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Donning.]

defs: To put on; to dress in; to invest one's self with.
quotes: Should I don this robe and trouble you.
At night, or in the rain, He dons a surcoat which he doffs at morn.
